sentences.text: Well, I was describing my grandmother's house were they went through like a tornado.
Everything in the house was destroyed.
I have one picture here, which I got from my grandma when I came to the States, which is about six by three, six by four.
You can see, still, the bag of the eggs in this little picture where they even had to destroy this, which was hanging on the wall in the-- in the living room or dining room.
I don't know which one.
But even to-- down to this little picture, they had to destroy it.
My grandma and my aunt climbed over fences in the back yard and through back alleys.
They made it to our house where, in fact, most of the Jewish community in that night-- at that night congregated, because, like I said, before, we were lucky.
Hardly was anything was destroyed.
Then, the next day, all the men between 16 and 60 were arrested and sent to various concentration camps at that time.
But most of them came, after three or four months would be released.
First, we started out with veterans from First World-- from World War I, then eventually they released more or less everybody who didn't have any police record.
They kept some who had a jaywalking record that-- longer than us or they didn't let them out at all anymore.
My brothers and I were lucky.
I don't know whether it was lucky or not in hind-- hindsight.
Because if we would have gone to the camp at that time, maybe we would have left Germany easier or earlier.
But the, the chief of police of  the little town called my mother and said, let your boys go in hiding.
I have to come to arrest them.
When he came, we naturally were gone.
My brother was hiding in a farm, which was outside of a town laying by itself.
The owner was a good friend of ours.
And he naturally right away took him in.
I went to my grandpa's house, because he was way over 60 at that time.
He was in his-- close to 80, actually.
And I saw they were safe there, but then all of a sudden the Gestapo came looking for my brother there.
And they went through the whole house looking all over.
And I was laying in a-- in a-- not-being-used bedroom under the bed way in the corner.
And they didn't-- they didn't see me.
Then after they left, I, I got a little bit scared and went to the next town, which, which was called Duren, which was, a bit further on, a bigger city where an aunt and a-- lived with her daughter.
The other daughter was already in Israel at that time.
It was called Palestine at that time.
She went in a youth transport.
And her husband, who was a traveling salesman, an international salesman, just happened to be at that time in Belgium, who naturally didn't come back when, when, when he heard about these things.
So I thought I was safe there.
In fact, I went into the streetcar after that-- I took the train.
I ran into the streetcar there.
And there was my cousin sitting there.
But I gave her right away a sign not to recognize me.
How did you do that?
What was the sign?
I looked at her and shook my head.
So she right away caught on.
When I went into their house, and was thinking I was safe there because there was just this one woman-- two women.
And after a while the Gestapo came there.
And again I was hiding behind the door.
And behind the-- behind that door there was a water tank, and I was squeezed behind the water tank.
And again, they didn't find me.
Then two days later, the chief of police called my mother and said, let the boys come home.
The arrest warrants are over.
So that was why we weren't at that time, neither my brothers or myself, arrested to go to the camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, this was the Kristallnacht.
And naturally after that, there came all the new laws out.
Jews couldn't have any more of their businesses.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Going back to Kristallnacht, a thousand excuses from the Nazis why they did it.
There was a young Jewish man whose parents were stranded between Germany and Poland.
His name was Grynszpan.
He went to the embassy in, in, in Paris and shot some third consul there, [NON-ENGLISH] employee.
And this is what the Nazis used as a-- as an excuse to start the Kristallnacht.
But to came back in the spring or early summer of '38, already every Jew had to declare how much his property he had.
Everybody who had more than-- his property was worth more than 5,000 marks had to declare already his bank book and everything what they owed.
So then af-- during-- after the Kristallnacht, they put a so-called Juden tax on all the Jews who had more than 5,000 marks.
So they were already preparing for that.
And everybody had said the Jews were in fault for the Kristallnacht when everybody-- [DRINKS] Excuse me.
Everybody had to give 25% of his wealth to the Nazis, because these-- the Jews were at fault for the Kristallnacht.
Now a funny thing is-- I don't know whether you realize that-- the 9th of November has a lot of meaning for German things.
First of all, in 1923, I think this was actually the reason the 9th of November was chosen for the-- for the Kristallnacht.
In 1923, Hitler had the first run-in with the police.
The Nazi Party had the first run-in with the police in Munich, in Munchen, about the-- the tried-- so-called putsch there.
They wanted to overthrow the city, the government.
And they had a clash with the police where-- I don't remember how many-- Nazis were killed at that time.
He had a song there, we'll never forget you, your deaths from the 9th of November.
The Nazis had that song.
But also the Weimar Republic went down on the 9th of November.
The 9th of November was a other deal.
He, he started his so-called SS, special troops, on the 9th of November.
And other things happened all the time in the German history what comes out to the 9th of November.
In fact, the Berlin Wall came down on the 9th of November.
I don't know why this always repeats itself, but this seems a date what, what has a significant meaning to Germany.
Well, after that, then everybody had to give up their jewelry or their, their fur coats.
Their stock had to be registered and was taken away.
The businesses where closed by the-- with the 31st of December in '38.
Jews couldn't have any more businesses.
Well, the business had to be closed from November-- from 9th of November till-- till December 31, or '38.
You had to, to close the business.
So what we did-- whatever we could to be sold to-- the stock was sold or given away or sold to a so-called-- to a friend of my grandpa who was-- he had the same business in a other city.
And the house we were living in we had to evacuate.
And like I said, before we moved to this Juden apartment in Cologne, and the Nazis made their headquarters in that house.
The house wasn't owned by, by my mother, because when we were ready to buy a house the Nazis didn't allow it.
So when she was going to buy one, we had a lot of-- not a lot-- but we had quite some-- owned quite some agriculture land and pastures and stuff like that.
And our other house were the stables for the business of my uncles were in.
This was all owned by my mother at that time.
But this is a different story.
I don't think we should go into that.
Well, we were living there, and then we were notified to-- we had to evac-- to evacuate the house.
By then, it on the-- already the spring of '39.
And we didn't then want to live in that  little town anymore, because we didn't have any business.
We thought we would be better off to live in a big city where not everybody knows each other, and where, you know, wouldn't be too harassed.
But the German machinery of knowing everything and the Gestapo didn't make that much difference.
Then all of a sudden, we had to wear the star.
So everybody-- there were too many people what knew you that you wouldn't wear the star, and would be afraid not to.
Well, when--
There were a few still there, which, which would say hello under their breath.
Most of them were afraid.
There were some which were openly-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what would you call it?
Openly friendly to you, which really defied the Nazis in some way.
But there were very, very few.
I mean, when they elected Hitler in '33 or even later when he had his one, one-party elections, there was-- 95% were for him.
And there really were 95% Nazis, because he promised them the whole world.
And he, he really got them out of the recession with, with starting to build his, his, his war machine.
Built the autobahn, which was also the war machine.
He built the so-called Siegfried Line, the armored line across-- around the border with France.
So he had people working there.
And he, he gave them work.
But the mark wasn't worth anything, because he was printing money like, like water.
Well, after '38, the conversation was how to get out of Germany.
We tried hundred different ways.
No, I wouldn't say 100.
We tried as many different ways as we could.
We got a-- America only took people in by quota number.
Well, our number was about two months away after the war broke out.
Naturally, they didn't let us out anymore.
The day in 1939 when Germany invaded Poland, the day to-- that was the 1st of September.
The 3rd of September, France and England declared war on Germany, and the same day, my brother and I got the entry visa to go into England.
Naturally, they didn't let us go.
Then we tried my uncle.
He had finally ended up in Seattle.
He was able to go to Cuba.
And then from Cuba, he was able to get himself the visa to come into the United States, because he had-- he smuggled jewelry and money into Belgium to an aunt.
And when he got the permission to go to Cuba, he picked it up there.
We smuggled money and jewelry into Belgium, but then the, the Germans-- walked into Belgium and sent the aunt away.
And I'll never know what happened to it.
Everything got lost.
Anyhow, my uncle went to Cuba.
And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To go to Cuba, you had to pull strings with the Cuban government over there, which he was able to do.
So we went on-- we booked passage to Cuba on the ship, which went after the one what Cuba refused to land, where the people were on the boat for months.
And then eventually went back to Holland.
And were laying for three weeks in front of Miami, and the American's didn't let them in.
Well, the next ship naturally didn't go anymore.
We had passage on there.
For Ecuador, we had to-- you had to bribe the consul in Hamburg to get your visa.
He would give you a visa if you bribed him, which we had done.
He called to come to Hamburg to pick up the visa.
My brother took the train at night without-- with taking his star off, because you need special permission to travel, which he didn't have.
And when he came to Hamburg to pick up the visa, they had to-- the night before, they had arrested the consul.
So this fell through.
Then, my uncle in Colombia, he got the papers ready for us to go to Colombia.
And like South America, there was a revolution every time.
And there was a revolution in, in Bogota.
And they burnt down the Parliament building, and our papers got burned.
So that fell through.
So as you can see, we tried so many things, and everything just, just didn't work out.
The rest of my mother's brothers and sisters all were able to emigrate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On my father's side, his sister and her husband stayed in Germany more or less to take care of the-- of his stepmother and my grandfather.
And depots-- I don't know where they were killed.
He was on the way.
And my uncle, the other uncle where I spoke from who was caught in Belgium that time, and his wife, both were killed in the camps, and I don't know where.
So this is just the close family, the, the, the immediate family.
Well, when we moved into Cologne, being nearly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had nearly completed my apprenticeship as a plumber.
In the beginning, one brother and I could work on, on a brick building that the Nazis were building.
The other boy was also Jewish.
We were separated from all the other workers and could work on the plumbing.
But that was only for a few months.
Then we were kicked out of there.
After that, we had to do labor work.
For instance, a lot of them were working on the autobahn.
The Jews, forced labor.
I was working on building air shelters in the-- in the middle of the city.
So as you can see, that was in 1939, 1940.
Hitler was already preparing for war with the whole world.
And this was what the Jews were doing.
You had to work forced labor, and you had restrictions, curfews.
You had curfews.
You had to have special ID cards.
You had to wear your star.
On the streetcar, you only could stand on the back-- in the platform on the back.
Your rations-- at that time, Germany already had shortages on food.
The rations for the Jews were half what the-- what the other population had.
The Jews didn't get any ration cards for clothing.
But like I said, my mother before, she had friends in that little town, and there was kind of a black market going on.
And some of their friends would bring extra food to our place.
And so we, we really had-- we didn't feel the emergency as bad as, as, as a lot of other people did.
Because living in that little town, all the farmers had butter, had eggs, had milk.
We would get-- they would kill cattles, and we would get some meat.
So this was the way we lived then.
And then this-- we lived in the Juden apartment with a other lady with two boys.
Her husband was a Polish citizen, and he was already shipped to Poland in '39.
He-- that is where this whole deal of the so-called Kristallnacht started.
This-- his parents were in that same situation there.
And then till October, October 31 or 30, I think, in 1941, we got-- two days before, we got a notice that we had to be at the railway station.
We could take 100 marks, which was nothing.
You could take one suitcase, and you could take bedding, but only as much as you could carry.
You had to report to the station in two days.
You would be sent to a so-called-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the east in the eastern part of Europe, which was Poland, where there would be cities.
There were cities being built where just Jews lived.
All your furniture would be shipped afterwards or stored for you.
And everything would be just like normal, only you would be living only under Jews.
So we didn't know anything what was going on.
No idea.
Before your telephones were taken away, and-- when they-- for instance, when they came and took the telephones away, right by the telephone we had a, a, a stamp collection which was very valuable.
My dad had already started it, and they took that away too.
They stole it, actually, because he wasn't supposed to.
But we wouldn't have dared to go to the Gestapo and say, the guy who picked up the phone, he picked up the-- he would say, you-- no, no, no German steals.
Only Jews steal, you know.
So this was the other deal what happened.
Well, then, we went-- we actually took more than 100 marks each.
We sewed the money into clothing and hiding it.
So we-- when we got-- so we came to the railway station, and we were put on trains.
And the train took off.
And we didn't have any idea where we were going.
And after two nights and three days, or two days, two and a half days, we-- the train all of a sudden came to a stop.
And as we got out, we walked through the mud, about a half a foot or a foot deep.
With your belongings, you were walking into an area, which was a slum area, which was encircled with, with barbed wires, guards all around it.
We didn't know what was happening.
You saw the people in there all in rags.
When we were in there, we found out what happening.
The people in rags were the Polish Jews which were put into the ghetto.
The city was large.
The German called it Litzmannstadt.
Already in '39, and they lived there already two years or so.
And the living conditions were just horrible.
The streets were just, just dirt and mud, and and there were open sewers running through it.
And you-- we didn't know what was hitting us.
We were the second transport from Cologne.
The other one went about 10, 14 days early.
With all the rest, we emptied an area in the slum area in this so-called ghetto.
And arriving every days were the transport of 1,000 each, two or three transports.
And they were pushed into this overpopulated area, which was overpopulated already.
They pushed in there within, I think, within about a space of a month, I think.
There were all of a sudden 20,000 people. 20 more thousand people pushed into this overcrowded area.
Well, a lot of the Polish Jews spoke-- excuse me-- spoke Yiddish.
Yiddish is a-- is a middle-aged German, actually.
So if they spoke slow, and a few spoke-- some of the German Jews spoke Yiddish.
A few of them spoke German.
So you would be able to communicate.
But there was no communication there, because all of a sudden there was, on one side, they came in, these people out of a-- of a normal lifestyle, more or less, even, even deprived in Germany.
But still you lived a normal life.
And then you came into an area where people were just beggars, and, and full of lice, full of dirt, hardly any water to wash.
Then you were put into a room.
The room we were in were about 30 people in a room.
I would say about 12 by 12, 14 by 14, men, women children, old people, young people, all in one room.
We, we didn't know what do.
We were just completely lost.
We, we didn't know.
We were just dumbfounded.
You tried to hang a, a sheet or something to get some privacy for the night, because you still were human.
You still were, were living a normal life.
But after a while, you-- after a few days, you already were-- you didn't see-- you didn't care whether somebody saw you undress or not anymore.
And the food you got was from the communal kitchen.
Was just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] water with some kind of a vegetable maybe boiled through it.
Because the food was so-- the food was, to start with, very, very minimal.
And all of a sudden, they had 20,000 more people to feed.
So they had to stretch it even more.
And this life in the ghettos was just horrible.
In the morn-- in the morning, you would see a hay wagon pulled by, by horse.
If you were lucky.
Most of the time, the people were pulling it, because the horse wouldn't live long.
It would-- somebody would kill it for meat or something.
So most-- I would say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 98% were pulled by people, the wagon.
And they would go through the street.
And in the streets, they would lay the people what died in the night.
And they would throw them onto this hay wagon like, like cords of wood.
And in this one room, there was no, no sanitation.
If you had to go to the bathroom, you had to go outside in this open ditch.
And everybody had diarrhea there right away.
We still had a little bread.
We hardly didn't touch that stuff what you got there.
And the conditions there were just horrible.
And everybody was in rags.
Then the European Jews came in looking like kings for them people.
Well, I was-- I myself was in there only for 10 days.
Then I was separated form my mother and brother.
I was sent in the so-called arbeitslager, the, the German DAF, Deutsche Arbeitsfront.
That was a camp which was run by the Gestapo.
